items.q: What does 'show, don't tell' encourage a writer to do?
items.correct: Reveal feelings and character through actions, senses and detail rather than stating them outright.
items.misconception: It means never describing anything directly at all.
items.random_wrong: It means the story must be told only in pictures.
items.why: Students misinterpret the technique as forbidding description rather than guiding indirect, vivid writing.

items.q: What is the purpose of structuring a narrative (exposition, climax, resolution)?
items.correct: To shape the reader's experience and build tension towards a satisfying conclusion.
items.misconception: Structure does not matter as long as the events are interesting.
items.random_wrong: Structure means putting events in alphabetical order.
items.why: Learners undervalue narrative structure, treating plot order as unimportant.

items.q: Why use sensory detail in descriptive writing?
items.correct: To create vivid images by appealing to sight, sound, smell, taste and touch.
items.misconception: Sensory detail just makes writing unnecessarily long.
items.random_wrong: Sensory detail means writing only about emotions.
items.why: Students see description as padding rather than a tool for immersive imagery.

items.q: How many senses to engage in description?
items.why: Correct answer: 5. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's New line. “Same line” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 5 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
